Dear Medical Records Custodian:
Please be advised that I represent the above named individual.
I hereby request copies of any and all medical/office records concerning my client for the
above referenced dates.
Please send me an itemized copy of your bill for professional services and keep me advised of
future billings and/or medical developments. Additionally, it would be extremely helpful if you
could hand write the total charges as well as any outstanding balance at the end of the billing
statement.
Enclosed please find a medical authorization signed by my client which entitles me to receive
this information, in accordance with KRS 422.317, at no charge for initial copies. If the free copy
has already been released, please notify my office of any and all fees prior to sending these
records.
Thank you for your cooperation in this matter.
Sincerely,

ED Provider Note (continued)
HPI:
Patient is a 79 yr/o female with a history of IDDMII, breast cancer, CKD, TIA, thyroid disease, gouts who
presents to the emergency department complaining of mechanical fall with resulting head injury. Indicates she
came to the hospital to sign and to have a fistula procedure done. She tripped over her shoe and fell
backwards hitting her head on the floor. She is on 81 mg ASA no other blood thinners. She denies preceding
chest pain, shortness of breath, headache. She denies loss of consciousness. Unsure of last tetanus shot
vaccination. Denies numbness, tingling, weakness in extremities. Denies neck pain. Denies vision changes.
Husband at bedside is concerned for hypoglycemia will get a point-of-care. Patient indicates other than mild
head soreness has no complaints.

RADIOLOGY REPORT FACILITY: NORTON HOSPITAL UNIT/AGE/GENDER: N.ED ER AGE:79 Y
SEX:F PATIENT NAME/DOB: PRINCE, BETTY, M [DOB-562] UNIT NUMBER: EP02001566 ACCOUNT
NUMBER: 10090237978 ACCESSION NUMBER: EPN20CT487035 EXAM: CT head without contrast.
ACCESSION NUMBER: EPN20CT487035 DATE: 7/14/2020 10:03 AM PROVIDED INDICATION: 79 years old
Female with history of fall. COMPARISON: None TECHNIQUE: Axial computed tomographic images were
obtained from the cranial vertex to the skull base without the use of intravenous contrast. Radiation dose reduction
techniques were used for the scan per the ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) protocol. Findings: Acute
subdural hemorrhage along the left parietal convexity, measuring 3-4 mm in greatest depth. The sulci and
ventricles are appropriate in size for the patient's age. There is no midline shift or mass effect. No acute territorial
infarcts. The visualized paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. The included orbits and intraorbital
contents are normal in appearance. There is no calvarial abnormality. Moderate soft tissue hematoma along the
superior left parietal scalp. IMPRESSION: Acute subdural hemorrhage along the left parietal convexity, measuring
3-4 mm in greatest depth. No acute fracture. There is no overlying soft tissue hematoma along the superior left
parietal scalp. Findings were discussed by Dr. Shah with Dr. Allinder via telephone at 10:10 AM on July 14, 2020.
Dictated by: Chandresh Shah, M.D. Images and Report reviewed and interpreted by: Chandresh Shah, M.D.

HPI: Pt is a 79 yr/o female who presents with closed head injury just PTA. Pt was upstairs at registration for
fistula placement by Dr. Dwivedi when her feet got tangled and she fell striking her head. No LOC. No AC use.
She has lac to her posterior scalp.

Head: 4cm lac to L posterior scalp
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally no wheezes

Extremities: No gross injury or deformity
Neuro: No focal deficits. Good strength bilaterally.

RADIOLOGY REPORT FACILITY: NORTON HOSPITAL UNIT/AGE/GENDER: N.4B IN AGE:79 Y
SEX:F PATIENT NAME/DOB: PRINCE, BETTY, M [DOB-562] UNIT NUMBER: EP02001566 ACCOUNT
NUMBER: 10090237978 ACCESSION NUMBER: EPN20CT490053 CT head without contrast, [DOB-503] at
0549 hours HISTORY: Left parietal subdural hematoma. TECHNIQUE: Multiple axial images were obtained
from the skullbase through the vertex. Images were obtained without IV contrast. The radiation dose reduction
technique was used to obtain ALARA for the exam. COMPARISON: July 14, 2020 FINDINGS: There is a stable
tiny left parietal subdural hematoma. No mass effect or midline shift is demonstrated. No new hemorrhage is seen.
There is no evidence hydrocephalus. The ventricles are stable in size. Bilateral lens extractions have been
performed. Orbits are unremarkable. Extensive calcific atherosclerotic vascular disease in the cavernous carotids.
The paranasal sinuses are clear. No mastoid or middle ear effusion is demonstrated. There is a stable small left
parietal scalp hematoma. IMPRESSION: 1. Stable tiny left parietal subdural hematoma. Dictated by: Durrett
Craddock, M.D. Images and Report reviewed and interpreted by: Durrett Craddock, M.D. <PS><Electronically
signed by: Durrett Craddock, M.D.> [DOB-502] 0712 D: [DOB-501] 0710 T: [DOB-500] 0710

left parietal scalp hematoma and air subcutaneous air secondary to the laceration is again noted. CONCLUSION:

Chief Complaint: fall
History of Present Illness:
Betty M Prince is a 79 yr/o female with history of breast cancer, insulin dependent diabetes mellitus, CKD stage
4, atrial fibrillation, coronary artery disease, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and gout who presented to the
emergency department after a mechanical fall. She was in the hospital for right arm venous shunt placement
when she tripped over her shoe, causing her to fall and she hit her head against the wall. She reports soreness
where she hit her head, but she denies headache, vision changes, loss of consciousness, unilateral numbness,
weakness, paresthesias, neck pain, dizziness, chest pain, shortness of breath, fever, chills, nausea, vomiting,
and diarrhea. There were no known aggravating or alleviating factors. She is on aspirin daily but is not on any
anticoagulation. Her husband states she loses her balance frequently, and that she has a walker that she uses
occasionally.

Betty M Prince is a 79 yr/o female with history of breast cancer, insulin dependent diabetes mellitus, CKD stage 4,
atrial fibrillation, coronary artery disease, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and gout who presented to the emergency
department after a mechanical fall and was found to have subdural hemorrhage.
1. Subdural hemorrhage: patient with mechanical fall causing SDH. Neurosurgery consulted in ED, discussed
with them. Hold aspirin/antiplatelet/anticoagulation. Neuro checks q2h, keep SBP<140, repeat head CT in 6
hours. Start Keppra 500mg BID per NES.
2. Insulin dependent diabetes mellitus: Hemoglobin A1C ordered. BG monitoring with SSI and resume home
diabetic regimen medications (insulin) along with NCS diet once ok with NES
3. Atrial fibrillation: Chads-vasc score 8. Not anticoagulated as outpatient secondary to previous severe

abdominal bleeding per husband. Close monitoring on telemetry for atrial fibrillation with RVR. Continue home
beta blocker and amiodarone
4. Coronary artery disease / hypertension / hyperlipidemia: currently asymptomatic. Continue home beta blocker,
statin, Ranexa; hold ASA due to subdural hemorrhage
5. Hypothyroidism: continue Synthroid
6. CKD stage 4: BUN 62, Cr 4.7, we do not have prior labs for comparison; but the patient was to undergo venous
shunt placement today for expected starting of dialysis soon; therefore suspect this is her baseline renal
function. Monitor volume status and lab values closely. Continue calcitriol
7. Hx breast cancer: continue Arimidex
8. Gout: continue allopurinol, but decrease dose based on renal function
9. DVT ppx: SCDs
Patient is high risk due to subdural hemorrhage.
Reason for admission: ICU monitoring, neurologic monitoring
Estimated length of stay > 2 days

History of Present Illness:
Pt is a 79 yrs old white female with known h/o hypertension,atrial fibrillation,breast ca,s/p axillary lymph
nodeDiabetes mellitus,ckd4 approaching stage 5.work up in the past included renal biopsy whtich showed,atn with
early diabetic nephropathy,this was attributed to a sample not representative of her ckd since pt over the last 1 1/2
yrs has progressive worsening of renal function.
Pt was registering with hospital for access placement by vascular surgery when she fell and hit her head.Pt didn't
lose her consciousness.No noted weakness,in extremities,ct scan with subdural hematoma.being followed by
neurosurgery.creatinine noted at 4.7,bun 62 which is close to her baseline.homemeds include aspirin.

urine output recently. Non report of any obstructive urinary symptoms (urgency, frequency, weak stream, straining
while urination). No h/o recurrent UTIs in the past.

NUMBER: 10090237978 ACCESSION NUMBER: EPN20CT489331 EXAMINATION: CT HEAD WITHOUT
CONTRAST. DATE:[DOB-470] INDICATION: subdural hemorrhage, 6 hour follow up scan COMPARISON: July
14, 2020, 0951 hours: TECHNIQUE: A routine noncontrast cranial CT scan was performed. Radiation dose
reduction techniques were utilized per ALARA protocol. FINDINGS: Allowing for differences in technique, scanner,
and plane of sectioning, there is no significant change in the small left parietal convexity subdural hemorrhage. No
associated hemorrhagic contusion or contrecoup injury has developed. Again, there is no skull fracture. The small
left parietal scalp hematoma and air subcutaneous air secondary to the laceration is again noted. CONCLUSION:
Stable small left parietal subdural hemorrhage. Dictated by: John Burger, M.D. Images and Report reviewed and
interpreted by: John Burger, M.D. <PS><Electronically signed by: John Burger, M.D.> [DOB-469] 1712 D:

Result Date: 7/14/2020
RADIOLOGY REPORT FACILITY: NORTON HOSPITAL UNIT/AGE/GENDER: N.ED ER AGE:79 Y
SEX:F PATIENT NAME/DOB: PRINCE, BETTY, M [DOB-562] UNIT NUMBER: EP02001566 ACCOUNT
NUMBER: 10090237978 ACCESSION NUMBER: EPN20CT487035 EXAM: CT head without contrast.
ACCESSION NUMBER: EPN20CT487035 DATE: 7/14/2020 10:03 AM PROVIDED INDICATION: 79 years old
Female with history of fall. COMPARISON: None TECHNIQUE: Axial computed tomographic images were
obtained from the cranial vertex to the skull base without the use of intravenous contrast. Radiation dose reduction
techniques were used for the scan per the ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) protocol. Findings: Acute
subdural hemorrhage along the left parietal convexity, measuring 3-4 mm in greatest depth. The sulci and
ventricles are appropriate in size for the patient's age. There is no midline shift or mass effect. No acute territorial
infarcts. The visualized paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. The included orbits and intraorbital
contents are normal in appearance. There is no calvarial abnormality. Moderate soft tissue hematoma along the
superior left parietal scalp. IMPRESSION: Acute subdural hemorrhage along the left parietal convexity, measuring
3-4 mm in greatest depth. No acute fracture. There is no overlying soft tissue hematoma along the superior left
parietal scalp. Findings were discussed by Dr. Shah with Dr. Allinder via telephone at 10:10 AM on July 14, 2020.
Dictated by: Chandresh Shah, M.D. Images and Report reviewed and interpreted by: Chandresh Shah, M.D.
<PS><Electronically signed by: Chandresh Shah, M.D.> [DOB-466] 1014 D: [DOB-465] 1009 T: [DOB-464] 1009

Neurosurgery Consult Note
Neurosurgery Attending: Dr. Harpring
Referring Provider: Dr. Riede
Chief Complaint: fall
HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Prince is a 79 yo female on 81 mg ASA with a PMHx significant for atrial fibrillation, CAD, HTN, HLD, DM,
CKD, breast CA, and gout who presented to the ED after a mechanical fall earlier today. She states she was in
registration for fistula placement to be performed by Dr. Dwivedi when she tripped over her feet and fell
backwards, striking the back of her head against the wall and floor. She reports soreness to posterior left scalp
where she hit her head. She denies any anticoagulant therapy at homeShe denies any loss of consciousness,
headache, visual changes, facial numbness or weakness, changes in speech, numbness in the extremities,
weakness in the extremities, dizziness, neck pain/low back pain, nausea or vomiting. She had a CT scan of the
head in the ED that revealed an acute subdural hematoma 3-4 mm in depth along the left parietal convexity and
Neurosurgery was consulted for further management.

RN attempted to call report to 4L at 1800. RN was put on hold, 4L RN stated in the background she didn't know
she was getting a patient.
RN spoke with Charge RN April, who stated she would speak to 4L Charge RN.
RN attempted to call report again at 1825 after speaking with Charge RN Stephanie. Spoke with Lea on 4L, who
said the RN taking the patient was in another room, and that the room the patient was supposed to go to was
still dirty with a patient in it. RN gave the extension to 4B. RN checked bed board and the room 4L02 was
marked clean and pending for Pt. RN called Charge RN Stephanie and informed her of the situation.

hours: TECHNIQUE: A routine noncontrast cranial CT scan was performed. Radiation dose reduction techniques were

is no significant change in the small left parietal convexity subdural hemorrhage. No associated hemorrhagic contusion

EPN20CT487035 DATE: 7/14/2020 10:03 AM PROVIDED INDICATION: 79 years old Female with history of fall.
COMPARISON: None TECHNIQUE: Axial computed tomographic images were obtained from the cranial vertex to the
skull base without the use of intravenous contrast. Radiation dose reduction techniques were used for the scan per the
ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) protocol. Findings: Acute subdural hemorrhage along the left parietal
convexity, measuring 3-4 mm in greatest depth. The sulci and ventricles are appropriate in size for the patient's age.
There is no midline shift or mass effect. No acute territorial infarcts. The visualized paranasal sinuses and mastoid air
cells are clear. The included orbits and intraorbital contents are normal in appearance. There is no calvarial
abnormality. Moderate soft tissue hematoma along the superior left parietal scalp. IMPRESSION: Acute subdural
hemorrhage along the left parietal convexity, measuring 3-4 mm in greatest depth. No acute fracture. There is no
overlying soft tissue hematoma along the superior left parietal scalp. Findings were discussed by Dr. Shah with Dr.
Allinder via telephone at 10:10 AM on July 14, 2020. Dictated by: Chandresh Shah, M.D. Images and Report reviewed
and interpreted by: Chandresh Shah, M.D. <PS><Electronically signed by: Chandresh Shah, M.D.> [DOB-148] 1014

 PLAN: · Labs reviewed, kidney function looks stable, does have underlying chronic kidney disease, noted seen by Dr. Ali
on [DOB-411], creatinine was around 4.3, so at baseline, · I will be more worried about her uremic symptoms, her husband thinks she is sleeping more, lack of energy, lack of
appetite, metallic taste, I feel she has uremic symptoms, I think she needs to be started hemodialysis sooner than
later, · I again had a long discussion with the patient and her husband regarding this, they want to wait till August, they
have a trip coming up, I did discuss about complication of avoiding hemodialysis including ongoing uremia,
hyperkalemia, acidosis, fortunately acidosis electrolytes has been stable. · Patient and her husband still wants to wait, and not initiate hemodialysis now, they want to complete the trip to
Arkansas, will be back in August 3 and willing to initiate hemodialysis, I did discuss about risk of postponing
hemodialysis including ongoing uremia, sometimes hyperkalemia acidosis and his cardiac event, · Will plan for the lab next week and follow-up with Dr. Ali, following Monday, · But I again highlighted my recommendation of initiating hemodialysis earlier the better, · If she gets admitted for initiation of hemodialysis, we can plan for AV fistula then, · D/w Dr Riede. · We will follow. · Low, k , protein diet. · D/w ICU RN. · Will d/w my associate Dr. Ali with his primary nephrologist.

HISTORY: Left parietal subdural hematoma. TECHNIQUE: Multiple axial images were obtained from the skullbase
through the vertex. Images were obtained without IV contrast. The radiation dose reduction technique was used to
obtain ALARA for the exam. COMPARISON: July 14, 2020 FINDINGS: There is a stable tiny left parietal subdural
hematoma. No mass effect or midline shift is demonstrated. No new hemorrhage is seen. There is no evidence
hydrocephalus. The ventricles are stable in size. Bilateral lens extractions have been performed. Orbits are
unremarkable. Extensive calcific atherosclerotic vascular disease in the cavernous carotids. The paranasal sinuses
are clear. No mastoid or middle ear effusion is demonstrated. There is a stable small left parietal scalp hematoma.
IMPRESSION: 1. Stable tiny left parietal subdural hematoma. Dictated by: Durrett Craddock, M.D. Images and Report
reviewed and interpreted by: Durrett Craddock, M.D. <PS><Electronically signed by: Durrett Craddock, M.D.>

EPN20CT487035 DATE: 7/14/2020 10:03 AM PROVIDED INDICATION: 79 years old Female with history of fall.
COMPARISON: None TECHNIQUE: Axial computed tomographic images were obtained from the cranial vertex to the
skull base without the use of intravenous contrast. Radiation dose reduction techniques were used for the scan per the
ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) protocol. Findings: Acute subdural hemorrhage along the left parietal
convexity, measuring 3-4 mm in greatest depth. The sulci and ventricles are appropriate in size for the patient's age.
There is no midline shift or mass effect. No acute territorial infarcts. The visualized paranasal sinuses and mastoid air
cells are clear. The included orbits and intraorbital contents are normal in appearance. There is no calvarial
abnormality. Moderate soft tissue hematoma along the superior left parietal scalp. IMPRESSION: Acute subdural
hemorrhage along the left parietal convexity, measuring 3-4 mm in greatest depth. No acute fracture. There is no
overlying soft tissue hematoma along the superior left parietal scalp. Findings were discussed by Dr. Shah with Dr.
Allinder via telephone at 10:10 AM on July 14, 2020. Dictated by: Chandresh Shah, M.D. Images and Report reviewed
and interpreted by: Chandresh Shah, M.D. <PS><Electronically signed by: Chandresh Shah, M.D.> [DOB-180] 1014

unremarkable. Extensive calcific atherosclerotic vascular disease in the cavernous carotids. The paranasal sinuses
are clear. No mastoid or middle ear effusion is demonstrated. There is a stable small left parietal scalp hematoma.
IMPRESSION: 1. Stable tiny left parietal subdural hematoma. Dictated by: Durrett Craddock, M.D. Images and Report
reviewed and interpreted by: Durrett Craddock, M.D. <PS><Electronically signed by: Durrett Craddock, M.D.>

hours: TECHNIQUE: A routine noncontrast cranial CT scan was performed. Radiation dose reduction techniques were

EPN20CT487035 DATE: 7/14/2020 10:03 AM PROVIDED INDICATION: 79 years old Female with history of fall.
COMPARISON: None TECHNIQUE: Axial computed tomographic images were obtained from the cranial vertex to the
skull base without the use of intravenous contrast. Radiation dose reduction techniques were used for the scan per the
ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) protocol. Findings: Acute subdural hemorrhage along the left parietal
convexity, measuring 3-4 mm in greatest depth. The sulci and ventricles are appropriate in size for the patient's age.
There is no midline shift or mass effect. No acute territorial infarcts. The visualized paranasal sinuses and mastoid air
cells are clear. The included orbits and intraorbital contents are normal in appearance. There is no calvarial
abnormality. Moderate soft tissue hematoma along the superior left parietal scalp. IMPRESSION: Acute subdural
hemorrhage along the left parietal convexity, measuring 3-4 mm in greatest depth. No acute fracture. There is no
overlying soft tissue hematoma along the superior left parietal scalp. Findings were discussed by Dr. Shah with Dr.
Allinder via telephone at 10:10 AM on July 14, 2020. Dictated by: Chandresh Shah, M.D. Images and Report reviewed
and interpreted by: Chandresh Shah, M.D. <PS><Electronically signed by: Chandresh Shah, M.D.> [DOB-366] 1014

trip coming up, I did discuss about complication of avoiding hemodialysis including ongoing uremia, hyperkalemia,
acidosis, fortunately acidosis electrolytes has been stable. · She was supposed to get AV fistula placement yesterday, for which she was here, follow-up neurosurgery but the
recommendation if she can get it done, she is going to be off the aspirin and Plavix,-maybe wait for another 1 to 2
weeks so that they can use. · I am thinking if she decides to do a dialysis and get admitted again for the tunnel catheter and initiating
hemodialysis, with the plan for AV fistula then to in 2 weeks, · D/w Dr Riede. · We will follow. · Low, k , protein diet. · D/w ICU RN. · Will d/w my associate Dr. Ali with his primary nephrologist.

53 - POWERSCRIBE NH POWERSCRIBE Unknown Unknown 11/03/11 1529 - Present
HISTORY: Left parietal subdural hematoma.
TECHNIQUE: Multiple axial images were obtained from the skullbase through the vertex. Images were obtained without IV
contrast. The radiation dose reduction technique was used to obtain ALARA for the exam.
COMPARISON: July 14, 2020
FINDINGS: There is a stable tiny left parietal subdural hematoma. No mass effect or midline shift is demonstrated. No new
hemorrhage is seen.
There is no evidence hydrocephalus. The ventricles are stable in size.
Bilateral lens extractions have been performed. Orbits are unremarkable. Extensive calcific atherosclerotic vascular disease in
the cavernous carotids. The paranasal sinuses are clear. No mastoid or middle ear effusion is demonstrated. There is a stable
small left parietal scalp hematoma.
IMPRESSION:
1. Stable tiny left parietal subdural hematoma.
Dictated by: Durrett Craddock, M.D.
Images and Report reviewed and interpreted by: Durrett Craddock, M.D.

53 - POWERSCRIBE NH POWERSCRIBE Unknown Unknown 11/03/11 1529 - Present
EXAMINATION: CT HEAD WITHOUT CONTRAST.
DATE:[DOB-230]
INDICATION: subdural hemorrhage, 6 hour follow up scan
COMPARISON: July 14, 2020, 0951 hours:
TECHNIQUE: A routine noncontrast cranial CT scan was performed. Radiation dose reduction techniques were utilized per
ALARA protocol.
FINDINGS: Allowing for differences in technique, scanner, and plane of sectioning, there is no significant change in the small left
parietal convexity subdural hemorrhage. No associated hemorrhagic contusion or contrecoup injury has developed. Again,
there is no skull fracture. The small left parietal scalp hematoma and air subcutaneous air secondary to the laceration is again
noted.
CONCLUSION: Stable small left parietal subdural hemorrhage.
Dictated by: John Burger, M.D.

RADIOLOGY REPORT

ACCOUNT NUMBER: 10090237978
ACCESSION NUMBER: EPN20CT487035
EXAM: CT head without contrast.
ACCESSION NUMBER: EPN20CT487035
DATE: 7/14/2020 10:03 AM
PROVIDED INDICATION: 79 years old Female with history of fall.
COMPARISON: None
TECHNIQUE:
Axial computed tomographic images were obtained from the cranial vertex to the skull base without the use of intravenous
contrast.
Radiation dose reduction techniques were used for the scan per the ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) protocol.
Findings:
Acute subdural hemorrhage along the left parietal convexity, measuring 3-4 mm in greatest depth.
The sulci and ventricles are appropriate in size for the patient's age.
There is no midline shift or mass effect.
No acute territorial infarcts.
The visualized paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear.
The included orbits and intraorbital contents are normal in appearance.
There is no calvarial abnormality.
Moderate soft tissue hematoma along the superior left parietal scalp.
IMPRESSION:
Acute subdural hemorrhage along the left parietal convexity, measuring 3-4 mm in greatest depth. No acute fracture. There is no
overlying soft tissue hematoma along the superior left parietal scalp.
Findings were discussed by Dr. Shah with Dr. Allinder via telephone at 10:10 AM on July 14, 2020.
Dictated by: Chandresh Shah, M.D.
Images and Report reviewed and interpreted by: Chandresh Shah, M.D.

hyperglycemia, results should be confirmed by repeat testing.
Patients with conditions that shorten erythrocyte survival, such as
recovery from acute blood loss, hemolytic anemia, kidney disease,
or the presence of unstable hemogloblins like HbSS, HbCC, and HbSC
may yield falsely decreased HbA1c test results. Iron deficiency may
yield falsely increased HbA1c test results.

Comment:
(note)
Anticoagulants may alter the results of Laboratory
Coagulation assays. New-generation anticoagulants such as
direct Thrombin inhibitors (Dabigatran/Pradaxa, Argatroban,
Bivalrudin) and direct/indirect factor Xa inhibitors
(Rivaroxaban/Xarelto,Apixaban/Eliquis, Danaparoid/Orgaran,
Fondaparinux/Arixtra) have been shown to affect the results
of Laboratory Coagulation assays, creating falsely elevated
or decreased values. Correlation with medication history is
advised.

Frequency: As needed per protocol PRN Rate/Duration: 100 mL/hr / 60 Minutes
Admin Instructions: Do not use if serum creatinine is greater than
2, and notify physician.
For Serum Potassium Level 3.5 to 3.9 mg/dL - Give 10 meq over 1
Hour x 2 Doses. Repeat Serum Potassium Level 2 Hours After Last
Dose.
For Serum Potassium Level 3.1 to 3.4 mg/dL - Give 10 meq over 1
Hour x 4 Doses. Repeat Serum Potassium Level 2 Hours After Last
Dose.
For Serum Potassium Level 3 mg/dL or less - Give 10 meq over 1
Hour x 6 Doses. Repeat Serum Potassium Level 2 Hours After Last
Dose.

Frequency: As needed per protocol PRN Rate/Duration: 103.3 mL/hr / 60 Minutes
Admin Instructions: Do Not Use Protocol If Serum Creatinine is
Greater Than 2 and NOTIFY MD.
*If Potassium is Greater Than 3.9 -AND- Sodium is Greater Than
145; NOTIFY MD for Phosphate Replacement Orders. ________________________ *Potassium Phosphate Will Be Used
When Potassium Less Than 4. __________________________
*Check Phosphorus Level (and Potassium Level if Potassium
Phosphate Administered) 2 Hrs Post Infusion and Repeat
Protocol if Necessary Up To a Maximum of 45 mmol Phosphate
per Day Unless Specified Otherwise by MD Orders.
*Order Daily Phosphorus Level While Protocol is Active.
For Phosphate = 2 - 3 mg/dL Give 1 Dose.
For Phosphate = 1.4 -1.9 mg/dL Give 2 Doses. For Phosphate = Less than 1.4 mg/dL Give 3 Doses.

Admin instructions: Do not use if serum creatinine is greater than 2, and notify physician.
Serum Potassium Level 3.5 To 3.9 mg/dL: Give 20 meq Now & 20 meq in Two
Hours. Repeat Serum Potassium Level 2 Hours After Last Dose.
Serum Potassium Level 3.1 To 3.4 mg/dL: Give 40 meq Now & 20 meq in Two
Hours. Repeat Serum Potassium Level 2 Hours After Last Dose.
For Serum Potassium Level Less Than 3 mg/dL : Use IV Critical Care
Potassium Replacement Protocol.

Admin instructions: Do not use if serum creatinine is greater than 2, and notify physician.
For Serum Potassium Level 3.5 to 3.9 mg/dL - Give 20 meq over 1 Hour x 1
Doses. Repeat Serum Potassium Level 2 Hours After Last Dose.
For Serum Potassium Level 3.1 to 3.4 mg/dL - Give 20 meq over 1 Hour x 2
Doses. Repeat Serum Potassium Level 2 Hours After Last Dose.
For Serum Potassium Level 3 mg/dL or less - Give 20 meq over 1 Hour x 3
Doses. Repeat Serum Potassium Level 2 Hours After Last Dose and Draw Serum
Magnesium Level. If Serum
Magnesium Level is Less Than 1.6 mg/dL, Then Institute the Critical Care
Magnesium Replacement Protocol.

Admin instructions: Do Not Use Protocol If Serum Creatinine is Greater Than 2 and NOTIFY MD.
*If Potassium is Greater Than 3.9 -AND- Sodium is Greater Than 145; NOTIFY
MD for Phosphate Replacement Orders. ___________________________
If Potassium is 4 or Greater, or
Patient is Currently Being Given
Potassium Chloride Runs:
Sodium Phosphate Will Be Used. __________________________
*Check Phosphorus Level (and Potassium Level if Potassium Phosphate
Administered) 2 Hrs Post Infusion and Repeat
Protocol if Necessary Up To a Maximum of 45 mmol Phosphate per Day Unless
Specified Otherwise by MD Orders.
*Order Daily Phosphorus Level While Protocol is Active.
For Phosphate = 2 - 3 mg/dL Give 1 Dose.
For Phosphate = 1.4 -1.9 mg/dL Give 2 Doses.
For Phosphate = Less than 1.4 mg/dL Give 3 Doses.

Acknowledged: Henson, Rachel C, RN 07/14/20 1525 for Placing Order Tubman, Paula, RN 07/16/20 0034 for D/C Order
Order comments: IF ON CRITICAL CARE POTASSIUM REPLACEMENT PROTOCOL: - Draw serum potassium level 2 hours after
last supplemental dose of potassium chloride (Nursing enter orders when needed) and continue with KCL supplementation per
potassium protocol. - If serum potassium level is less than 2.5 mmol/L, call MD. Also draw serum magnesium level and initiate MG
protocol if serum MG level is less than 1.6 (Nursing enter orders when needed) IF ON CRITICAL CARE MAGNESIUM
REPLACEMENT PROTOCOL: - Draw daily AM serum magnesium, potassium & creatinine levels if critical care Magnesium is given
and continue with magnesium supplementation per protocol (Nursing enter orders as needed) - If magnesium level was less than 1.3
mg/dl, also draw serum magnesium and potassium levels 4 hours after last MG supplement and continue supplementation per MG
protocol (Nursing enter orders as needed)

Questionnaire
Question
Questionnaire
Question Answer
Criteria for PRN ECG: If initial EKG is not diagnostic but patient remains symptomatic
and there is clinical suspicion for ACS, subsequent EKGs
every 15 to 30 minutes for the first hour.
Nursing instructions: Enter ECG order in Epic EACH time ECG is done. Results will
not cross over w/o an Epic order. Use "per active conditional
order" mode.
Answer
Criteria for PRN ECG: acute chest pain / ACS, dysrhythmias or acute ST/T wave
changes, respiratory or hemodynamic instability.
Nursing instructions: Enter ECG order in Epic EACH time ECG is done. Results will
not cross over w/o an Epic order. Use "per active conditional
order" mode.

Order comments: - Institute for serum phosphate level less than 3 mg/dL - Do NOT use if serum creatinine is greater than 2 and
Notify MD - If potassium is greater than 3.9 -and- sodium is greater than 145, then notify MD for phosphate replacement orders. -
Use Potassium Phosphate when potassium is less than 4. - Use Sodium Phosphate when potassium is 4 or more -or- patient is
currently being administered Potassium Chloride runs. NOTE: 10 mmol Phosphate = 14.7 mEq of potassium or 13.3 mEq of Sodium

blood glucose within 1 hour of admission to critical care unit. - If INITIAL glucose is greater than 150, repeat blood glucose in 1 hour.

Order comments: FREQUENCY: Every 6 hrs for NPO / TPN / Tube Feeding patients -or- ACHS for eating patients (Target range
for glucose: 100 - 150 mg/dL) - If all results are in target range for 48 hrs, then discontinue POCT testing. PATIENTS that are NOT
OPEN HEART: - If blood glucose is greater than 150 for 2 consecutive readings, then initiate "Critical Care Intermittent
Subcutaneous Insulin Standing Orders" & discontinue this order. FOR OPEN HEART PATIENTS: - If blood glucose is greater than
150 for 2 consecutive readings -OR- greater than 180 at any one time, initiate "Critical Care GMAT IV Insulin Infusion Standing
Orders" and discontinue this order. FOR ANY PATIENT: - If blood glucose is Less Than or Equal to 70, initiate "Adult Hypoglycemia
Standing Orders"..
Instance released by: Henson, Rachel C, RN (auto-released)
7/14/2020 3:14 PM

Order comments: - Institute for SERUM MAGNESIUM LEVEL IS LESS THAN 1.6 mg/dL. - Do NOT use protocol if serum creatinine
is greater than 2 -AND- notify physician. - Do NOT exceed maximum magnesium sulfate IV infusion rate of 1 gram per hour.

Goal: Fluid and electrolyte balance are achieved/maintained (Resolved)
Dates: Start: 07/14/20 Expected End: 07/17/20 End: 07/16/20
Description: Assess and monitor vital signs (orthostatic vitals if applicable), fluid intake and output, urine color,
labs, skin turgor, mucous membranes, jugular venous distention, edema, circumference of
edematous extremities and abdominal girth, respiratory status, and mental status. Monitor for
signs and symptoms of hypovolemia (tachycardia, rapid breathing, decreased urine output,
postural hypotension, confusion, syncope). Monitor for signs and symptoms of hypervolemia
(strong rapid pulse, shortness of breath, difficulty breathing lying down, crackles heard in lung
fields, edema). Collaborate with interdisciplinary team and initiate plan and interventions as
ordered.
Disciplines: Interdisciplinary

Determine patient's food preferences and provide high-protein, high-caloric foods as appropriate.

Description: Assess and monitor vital signs, skin (color, moisture, integrity, turgor), respiratory status, urinary

hospital protocol/policy. Collaborate with interdisciplinary team and initiate plan and interventions
as ordered.

Topic: Hyperglycemia Management (Resolved)
* 4 to 6 ounces of fruit juice
* 3 to 4 glucose tablets
* 3 to 5 peppermint hard candies
* 8 to 10 small flavored Life Savers candies
* 4 to 6 ounces of a regular soft drink
Treatment if mild to moderate hypoglycemia: treat blood glucose level of less than 70 (or otherwise directed by healthcare
provider) with 15 grams of rapid acting carbohydrate. Recheck blood glucose in 15 minutes. If needed, treat with additional
15 grams of rapid acting carbohydrate.
Treatment of severe hypoglycemia: Use glucagon emergency kit.
Point: Prevention of Hypoglycemia (Resolved)
management and learning to detect and treat hypoglycemia early before it
gets worse.

Explain that when blood sugar levels are too low, eating something with carbohydrates can help.
Encourage them to keep high-carbohydrate snacks on hand.
Blood glucose levels can be raised quickly by eating or drinking something that contain 15 grams of glucose or carbohydrates
containing glucose. The following are a few good options:
Educate patient/family/caregiver on the treatment for hypoglycemia. Include teaching the use of glucagon. Have them
return demonstrate giving glucagon in a simulated environment.

Description:
Educate patient/family/caregiver about DKA. Discuss possible reasons for DKA; possible signs and symptoms of DKAfruity breath, Kussmauls breathing, increased thirst, increased urine output, urine positive for large ketones, dehydrated,
nausea/vomiting; and ways to prevent DKA-sick day management, taking medication(s) as prescribed, and following diet.
Discuss management and treatment of DKA, including importance of slow decrease in blood sugar. Discuss probable
disease process, including plan of care and treatment (s).
Educate patient/family/caregiver that patient may have nothing to eat or drink. Have patient/family/caregiver inform staff of
time of last food or drink.

Point: Teach Back 3 Reasons Fall Prevention is Important (Resolved)
1. Falls for the most part are preventable
2. Falls can result in injury
3. Falls can make your hospital stay longer
2. Habilitation or rehabilitation techniques to help the patient reach maximum independence.
Point: Teach Back 4 Actions to Take to Prevent Falls (Resolved)
1. Learn about your fall risk factors
2. Call first for help- use the call light
3. Wait for help before your get out of bed or up from a chair
4. Wear non-slip footwear (yellow/red socks)

Third Party Apps and Epic
Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record
for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.
For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.
Norton Care Call
If you are going home after leaving the hospital you will receive a Norton Care Call
* Within 48 hours ofdischarge, you should receive an automated phone call from [PHONE-328] (Caller ID will read
Norton Care Call)
* You will be asked a few questions about your health and progress. Please answer the questions using the phone
keypad.
* Based on your answers, a Norton Healthcare team member may call you to offer help and other instructions.
Medical Records
Request medical records through MyNortonChart by using the Ask Customer Service feature or complete an
authorization form at https://medicalrecordrequest.nortonhealthcare.org
Requests are processed within 7 business days of receipt.

Instructions
What you need to know about your Norton Care Call
* Please expect an automated phone call from [PHONE-329] so we can see howyou are doing as you recover,
and provide help if needed. The Caller ID will read: Norton Care Call
You will receive thecall from our automated system within 48 hours after leaving the hospital.
You will be asked a few questions about your health and progress. Please answer the questions using the
phone keypad.
Based on your answers, a Norton Healthcare team member may call you back to offer help and instructions.
The goal ofthe Norton Care Call is to make sure patients are safe and have theresources needed to care for themselves.
We appreciate your time completing the survey to help us recognize staff members as well as adjust anything that may
have fallen short of your expectations. Thank you for allowing us to care for you during your visit to Norton Healthcare.
We hope that you will choose us for your future health care needs.

Your Rights asaHospital Inpatient:
¢ You can receive Medicare covered services. This includes medically necessary hospital services and
Services you may need after you are discharged, if ordered by your doctor. You havea right toknow
about these services, who will pay for them, and where you can get them.
© You can be involved in any decisions about your hospital stay.
¢ You can reportanyconcerns you have about the quality of care you receive toyour QIO at:KEPRO jdischarge [PHONE-333]. The QIOis the independent Teviewer authorized by Medicare toreview the decision to you.
You can speak with your doctor or other hospital Staff ifyou have concerns about being discharged.
Your Right to Appeal Your Hospital Discharge;
* You havethe right to an immediate, independent medical review (appeal) of the decision todischarge
you from the hospital. If you dothis, you will not have to pay for the services you receive during the
appeal (except for charges like copays and deductibles). ;
e wot, toese 10 appeal, you and the reviewer will each receive a copy ofa detailed explanation about
why your covered hospital stay should not Continue. You will receive this detailed notice only after you request anappeal
© If theQIO finds that you are not ready to be discharged from the hospital, Medicare will contime to Cover your hospital services,
° If theQIO agrees services should no longer be covered after thedischarge date, neither Medicare nor
your Medicare heaith plan will pay for your hospital Stay after noon ofthe day after the QIO notifies you
ofits decision If you Stop services no later than that time, you will avoid fnancial lability.
* Ifyoudonotappeal, youmay have to pay for any services you receive after your discharge date,
See page 2 of this notice for more information.

IN NORTON
HEALTHCARE
Real people. Remarkable care.
Patient Name: Prince,Betty M Department of Health & Human Services
Patient MRNumber: EP02001566 Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services
Acct Number: 202639418 OMB APPROVAL NO. 0938-1019
Physician: Dwivedi, Amit J., MD
Important Message from Medicare
Your Rights as a Hospital Inpatient:
You can receive Medicare covered services. This includes medically necessary hospital services and services you
may need after you are discharged, if ordered by your doctor. You have a right toknow about these services, who
will pay for them, and where you can getthem.
You can be involved in any decisions about your hospital stay.
You can report any concerns you have about the quality ofcare you receive to your QIO at: KEPRO 1-813-280-
8256. The QIO is the independent reviewer authorized by Medicare to review the decision to discharge you.
You can work with the hospital to prepare for your safe discharge and arrange for services you may need after
you leave the hospital. When you no longer need inpatient hospital care, your doctor or the hospital staff will inform
you of your planned discharge date.
You can speak with your doctor or other hospital staff ifyou have concerns about being discharged.
Your Right to Appeal Your Hospital Discharge:
You have the right to an immediate, independent medical review (appeal) of thedecision to discharge you from
the hospital. If you do this, you will not have to pay for the services you receive during the appeal (except for
charges like copays anddeductibles).
If you choose to appeal, the independent reviewer will ask for your opinion. The reviewer also will look at
your medical records and/or other relevantinformation. You do nothave to prepare anything in writing, but
you have theright to do so if you wish.
Ifyou choose to appeal, you andthe reviewer will each receive acopy of adetailed explanation about why
your covered hospital stay should not continue. You will receive this detailed notice only after you request an
appeal.
If the QIO finds that you are not ready to be discharged from the hospital, Medicare will continue to cover
your hospital services.
If theQIO agrees services should no longer be covered after thedischarge date, neither Medicare nor your
Medicare health plan will pay for your hospital stay after noon of the day after the QIO notifies you ofits
decision. If you stop services nolater than that time, you will avoid financial liability.
If you donot appeal, you may have to pay for any services you receive after your discharge date.
See page 2 ofthis notice for more information.
Form CMS 10065-IM (Exp. [DOB-361]) OMBapproval 0938-1019
How toAsk For anAppeal of your Hospital Discharge

CONDITIONS OF ADMISSION AND
AUTHORIZATION FOR TREATMENT □ Norton Children's Hospital □ Norton Hospital □ Norton Audubon Hospital □ Norton Women’s and Children’s Hospital □ Norton Brownsboro Hospital □ Norton Children’s Medical Center
I. CONSENT TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTS, PROCEDURES, AND MEDICAL TREATMENT:
I voluntarily consent to care that involves routine diagnostic tests, procedures, and medical treatment as prescribed by my physician and performed by the facility. No guarantee has
been given by anyone as to the results that may be obtained. This consent includes testing for Hepatitis, Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) or any other blood-borne infectious
diseases, if ordered for diagnostic purposes or due to an occupational exposure. I consent to photographing, videotaping, video monitoring and audio devices for medical, scientific,
safety or education purposes as well as for the purposes of identification. I understand that these will be stored in a secure manner that will protect my privacy. Images that identify me
will only be released when authorized by me or as needed to provide for my continued medical treatment or identification.
II. OTHER PRACTITIONERS AND SUPPLIERS OF SERVICES:
I understand and acknowledge that I may require the services of physicians, groups of physicians or other practitioners (such as nurses and physician assistants) who are not
employees of the facility, including, but not limited to, anesthesiologists, radiologists, emergency department physicians, certified registered nurse anesthetists, and other practitioners. I also understand that residents, fellows, medical students, and students of various health care fields may be involved in my care and that they are not employees of the
facility. I agree that the signing of this document does not in any way imply that Norton Healthcare is responsible for or assumes any liability for the activities of any such physicians or practitioners who are not its employees. When a physician, other practitioner or supplier of services bills me directly, I agree to pay the bill. I agree that the provisions regarding the
release of information and assignment of benefits set out in paragraphs III and V below shall apply not only to the facility but also to such physicians, practitioners and suppliers of
services and their claims for payment.
III. RELEASE OF INFORMATION:
I authorize the facility to notify my referring and treating physician(s), of my admission to the facility and to release any information about me as requested by such physician(s). I
authorize the facility and any other holder of medical or other information about me including medical information concerning drug related conditions, alcoholism, psychological
conditions, psychiatric conditions, and/or blood-borne infectious diseases, to release to the Social Security Administration, its intermediaries and its carriers, State agencies, the
review organization employed by my employer, or the employer of the insured member of my family, or any private third-party payor, as appropriate, any information required for the
completion of any claim for benefits arising out of services rendered to me either on an inpatient or outpatient basis. I further authorize the release of my social security number to the
manufacturer of any implanted medical device in accordance with the Medical Device Tracking Act of the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). I authorize the release of my HIV test
results to healthcare personnel in the event of an occupational exposure. If my injury is work-related, I authorize the facility and any other holder of medical or other information about
me to release from my medical record any information as requested by the company and/or its workers compensation administrator as indicated below:
COMPANY________________________________________________________________________ ADDRESS__________________________________________ IV. (initial)
THE FACILITY IS NOT RESPONSIBLE FOR LOSS OF PERSONAL ARTICLES:
I understand that the facility maintains a safe for the safekeeping of money and valuables and the facility shall not be liable for the loss of or damage to any money, jewelry,
documents, or any other items of personal property, unless deposited in the facility safe for safekeeping. Furthermore, I accept full responsibility for items of personal property kept in
my possession.

V. ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS:
I agree to the assignment of all third-party benefits to the facility and agree to pay the facility for all charges not covered by third-party payors. In the case of outpatient services I agree
that this document shall remain in full force and effect until specifically revoked by me in writing. Any sums not paid by a third-party payor are my obligation.
VI. RELEASE OF INSURANCE INFORMATION TO FACILITY:
I agree to the release by my insurance carrier to the facility and the facility's billing service of any eligibility and utilization data concerning my insurance coverage which the facility and/or its billing
supplier may require. I authorize the facility to file an appeal of a payment denial and/or request a hearing regarding any denial of payment for treatment provided to me. I further authorize the facility
to present proof, utilize facility records and my records and documentation for appeals and hearings.
VII. PAYMENT OF FACILITY BILLS WHEN DUE:
I am liable for the payment of the account of the facility and I am obligated to pay the account of the facility, in accordance with the regular rates and terms of the facility. If my injury is work-related, I am further obligated to pay the account of the facility in the event my employer or its third-party administrator denies payment for workers compensation. It is understood that the uninsured portion of bills are payable at the end of each week and in full upon discharge from the facility. I understand that after reasonable notice, delinquent
accounts may be turned over to a collection agency and/or an attorney for collection. Should it be necessary to place this account in the hands of an attorney for collection, I agree to
pay the costs of collection, including any court costs and a reasonable fee to the attorney employed by the facility to collect the account.
I agree that the facility, its employees, agents, attorneys and collection agencies, may call the cell number I provide for any reason, including but not limited to inquiring about the
status and collection of my account. I also expressly authorize that each of them may use any mechanism, including but not limited to automatic dialing, artificial and prerecorded
voice systems. VIII.HIV/AIDS INFORMATION: I acknowledge receipt of an HIV/AIDS informational brochure.
IX. SMOKING CESSATION COUNSELING (18 YEARS AND OLDER): I acknowledge receipt of smoking cessation counseling informational handout.
X. PRIVACY NOTICE: I acknowledge that a copy of Norton's Notice of Privacy Practices has been made available to me.
XI. PATIENT BILL OF RIGHTS: I acknowledge that a copy of the Patient Bill of Rights has been made available to me.
The undersigned agrees that a copy of this release and assignment may be used in place of the original copy. The undersigned certifies that he/she has read and agrees to this
agreement and has received a copy. The undersigned certifies that the patient is unable to consent or is a minor and the undersigned hereby consents to the above as indicated below.
If the patient is not the insured, or if the patient does not pay the account in full, the undersigned agrees to be financially responsible for the charges detailed above, individually or jointly with the
patient.

CT head without contrast,
[DOB-391] at 0953 hours
HISTORY: Left parietal subdural hematoma.
TECHNIQUE: Multiple axial images were obtained from the skullbase through the vertex. Images were obtained without IV
contrast. The radiation dose reduction technique was used to obtain ALARA for the exam.
COMPARISON: CT of the head performed on July 15, 2020
FINDINGS: The left parietal subdural hematoma has resolved.
There is no evidence of acute intracranial hemorrhage, hydrocephalus, or mass-effect. The brain parenchyma is
unremarkable. The ventricles are normal size and shape.
Bilateral lens extractions have been performed. The orbits and pituitary fossa are within normal limits. The visualized
portions of the paranasal sinuses and mastoids are clear. The skull base and calvarium are unremarkable.

past 3 weeks?

Important Message from Medicare
Your Rights as a Hospital Inpatient:
You can receive Medicare covered services. This includes medically necessary hospital services and services you
may need after you are discharged, if ordered by your doctor. You have a right toknow about these services, who
will pay for them, and where you can getthem.
You can be involved in any decisions about your hospital stay.
You can report any concerns you have about the quality ofcare you receive to your QIO at: KEPRO 1-813-280-
8256. The QIO is the independent reviewer authorized by Medicare to review the decision to discharge you.
You can work with the hospital to prepare for your safe discharge and arrange for services you may need after
you leave the hospital. When you no longer need inpatient hospital care, your doctor or the hospital staff will inform
you of your planned discharge date.
You can speak with your doctor or other hospital staff ifyou have concerns about being discharged.
Your Right to Appeal Your Hospital Discharge:
You have the right to an immediate, independent medical review (appeal) of thedecision to discharge you from
the hospital. If you do this, you will not have to pay for the services you receive during the appeal (except for
charges like copays anddeductibles).
If you choose to appeal, the independent reviewer will ask for your opinion. The reviewer also will look at
your medical records and/or other relevantinformation. You do nothave to prepare anything in writing, but
you have theright to do so if you wish.
Ifyou choose to appeal, you andthe reviewer will each receive acopy of adetailed explanation about why
your covered hospital stay should not continue. You will receive this detailed notice only after you request an
appeal.
If the QIO finds that you are not ready to be discharged from the hospital, Medicare will continue to cover
your hospital services.
If theQIO agrees services should no longer be covered after thedischarge date, neither Medicare nor your
Medicare health plan will pay for your hospital stay after noon of the day after the QIO notifies you ofits
decision. If you stop services nolater than that time, you will avoid financial liability.
If you donot appeal, you may have to pay for any services you receive after your discharge date.
See page 2 ofthis notice for more information.
Form CMS 10065-IM (Exp. [DOB-398]) OMBapproval 0938-1019

CONDITIONS OF ADMISSION AND
AUTHORIZATION FOR TREATMENT □ Norton Children's Hospital □ Norton Hospital □ Norton Audubon Hospital □ Norton Women’s and Children’s Hospital □ Norton Brownsboro Hospital □ Norton Children’s Medical Center
I. CONSENT TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTS, PROCEDURES, AND MEDICAL TREATMENT:
I voluntarily consent to care that involves routine diagnostic tests, procedures, and medical treatment as prescribed by my physician and performed by the facility. No guarantee has
been given by anyone as to the results that may be obtained. This consent includes testing for Hepatitis, Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) or any other blood-borne infectious
diseases, if ordered for diagnostic purposes or due to an occupational exposure. I consent to photographing, videotaping, video monitoring and audio devices for medical, scientific,
safety or education purposes as well as for the purposes of identification. I understand that these will be stored in a secure manner that will protect my privacy. Images that identify me
will only be released when authorized by me or as needed to provide for my continued medical treatment or identification.
II. OTHER PRACTITIONERS AND SUPPLIERS OF SERVICES:
I understand and acknowledge that I may require the services of physicians, groups of physicians or other practitioners (such as nurses and physician assistants) who are not
employees of the facility, including, but not limited to, anesthesiologists, radiologists, emergency department physicians, certified registered nurse anesthetists, and other practitioners. I also understand that residents, fellows, medical students, and students of various health care fields may be involved in my care and that they are not employees of the
facility. I agree that the signing of this document does not in any way imply that Norton Healthcare is responsible for or assumes any liability for the activities of any such physicians or practitioners who are not its employees. When a physician, other practitioner or supplier of services bills me directly, I agree to pay the bill. I agree that the provisions regarding the
release of information and assignment of benefits set out in paragraphs III and V below shall apply not only to the facility but also to such physicians, practitioners and suppliers of
services and their claims for payment.
III. RELEASE OF INFORMATION:
I authorize the facility to notify my referring and treating physician(s), of my admission to the facility and to release any information about me as requested by such physician(s). I
authorize the facility and any other holder of medical or other information about me including medical information concerning drug related conditions, alcoholism, psychological
conditions, psychiatric conditions, and/or blood-borne infectious diseases, to release to the Social Security Administration, its intermediaries and its carriers, State agencies, the
review organization employed by my employer, or the employer of the insured member of my family, or any private third-party payor, as appropriate, any information required for the
completion of any claim for benefits arising out of services rendered to me either on an inpatient or outpatient basis. I further authorize the release of my social security number to the
manufacturer of any implanted medical device in accordance with the Medical Device Tracking Act of the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). I authorize the release of my HIV test
results to healthcare personnel in the event of an occupational exposure. If my injury is work-related, I authorize the facility and any other holder of medical or other information about
me to release from my medical record any information as requested by the company and/or its workers compensation administrator as indicated below:
COMPANY________________________________________________________________________ ADDRESS__________________________________________ IV. (initial)
THE FACILITY IS NOT RESPONSIBLE FOR LOSS OF PERSONAL ARTICLES:
I understand that the facility maintains a safe for the safekeeping of money and valuables and the facility shall not be liable for the loss of or damage to any money, jewelry,
documents, or any other items of personal property, unless deposited in the facility safe for safekeeping. Furthermore, I accept full responsibility for items of personal property kept in
my possession.

V. ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS:
I agree to the assignment of all third-party benefits to the facility and agree to pay the facility for all charges not covered by third-party payors. In the case of outpatient services I agree
that this document shall remain in full force and effect until specifically revoked by me in writing. Any sums not paid by a third-party payor are my obligation.
VI. RELEASE OF INSURANCE INFORMATION TO FACILITY:
I agree to the release by my insurance carrier to the facility and the facility's billing service of any eligibility and utilization data concerning my insurance coverage which the facility and/or its billing
supplier may require. I authorize the facility to file an appeal of a payment denial and/or request a hearing regarding any denial of payment for treatment provided to me. I further authorize the facility
to present proof, utilize facility records and my records and documentation for appeals and hearings.
VII. PAYMENT OF FACILITY BILLS WHEN DUE:
I am liable for the payment of the account of the facility and I am obligated to pay the account of the facility, in accordance with the regular rates and terms of the facility. If my injury is work-related, I am further obligated to pay the account of the facility in the event my employer or its third-party administrator denies payment for workers compensation. It is understood that the uninsured portion of bills are payable at the end of each week and in full upon discharge from the facility. I understand that after reasonable notice, delinquent
accounts may be turned over to a collection agency and/or an attorney for collection. Should it be necessary to place this account in the hands of an attorney for collection, I agree to
pay the costs of collection, including any court costs and a reasonable fee to the attorney employed by the facility to collect the account.
I agree that the facility, its employees, agents, attorneys and collection agencies, may call the cell number I provide for any reason, including but not limited to inquiring about the
status and collection of my account. I also expressly authorize that each of them may use any mechanism, including but not limited to automatic dialing, artificial and prerecorded
voice systems. VIII.HIV/AIDS INFORMATION: I acknowledge receipt of an HIV/AIDS informational brochure.
IX. SMOKING CESSATION COUNSELING (18 YEARS AND OLDER): I acknowledge receipt of smoking cessation counseling informational handout.
X. PRIVACY NOTICE: I acknowledge that a copy of Norton's Notice of Privacy Practices has been made available to me.
XI. PATIENT BILL OF RIGHTS: I acknowledge that a copy of the Patient Bill of Rights has been made available to me.
The undersigned agrees that a copy of this release and assignment may be used in place of the original copy. The undersigned certifies that he/she has read and agrees to this
agreement and has received a copy. The undersigned certifies that the patient is unable to consent or is a minor and the undersigned hereby consents to the above as indicated below.
If the patient is not the insured, or if the patient does not pay the account in full, the undersigned agrees to be financially responsible for the charges detailed above, individually or jointly with the
patient.

[DOB-478] - Admission (Discharged) in NH Periop Services (continued)
OP Notes (continued)
ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: Per Anesthesia.
IV FLUIDS ADMINISTERED: Per Anesthesia.
DRAINS: No drains.
SPECIMENS: No specimens.
COMPLICATIONS: No complications.
PREOPERATIVE INDICATIONS: Betty is a 79-year-old patient who needs
permanent access placement. Decision was made to do a brachiocephalic
fistula. Intraoperative ultrasound showed that there was a high
bifurcation of the brachial artery, so we decided to use one of the
arteries to create the fistula.
DESCRIPTION OF PROCEDURE: The patient was laid in supine position, parts
were prepped with Betadine, draped in a sterile fashion as usual. A
transverse incision was made in the right upper extremity. Skin and
subcutaneous tissue were incised. Cephalic vein was identified. Branches
were tied with the help of 3-0 and 4-0 silk sutures and divided. Distal
part of the cephalic vein was divided and tied with the help of 2-0 silk
sutures. The brachial artery was then exposed underneath the fascia. A
branch of the brachial artery appears to be radial artery. Anastomosis was
performed between the cephalic vein and the radial artery using 7-0 Prolene
sutures in a continuous fashion. Hemostasis was checked. Irrigation was
done with saline. Subcutaneous tissue was approximated with 3-0 Vicryl
sutures in a continuous fashion. Skin was closed with 4-0 Vicryl sutures
in a continuous subcuticular fashion. A dressing was applied. The patient
had a thrill in the fistula at the end of the procedure. The patient
tolerated the procedure well, was extubated, transferred to recovery room
with stable vital signs.
Amit J Dwivedi, MD
D: [DOB-479] 14:53:59
T: [DOB-480] 15:28:42/MODL

Real people. Remarkable care.
Third Party Apps and Epic
Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for
their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.
For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.
Norton Care Call
If you are going home after leaving the hospital you will receive a Norton Care Call
* Within 48 hours ofdischarge, you should receive an automated phone call from [PHONE-539] (Caller ID will read
Norton Care Call)
* You will be asked a few questions about your health and progress. Please answer the questions using the phone
keypad.
* Based on your answers, a Norton Healthcare team member may call you to offer help and other instructions.
Medical Records
Request medical records through MyNortonChart by using the Ask Customer Service feature or complete an authorization
form at https://medicalrecordrequest.nortonhealthcare.org
Requests are processed within 7 business days of receipt.

Instructions
Anesthesia Post Op Instructions
You will need an adult with you for the first 24 hours after surgery. Go directly home and rest.
Do NOT perform any of the following activities for 24hours after surgery or while on narcotic pain medication:
* Go to work
* Drink alcohol
* Drive
* Operate Machinery
* Make any important decisions
* Sign any legal documents
+ Eat spicy, greasy foods (begin with a bland diet)
Do NOT perform the following activities until instructed to do so by your surgeon:
* Swim or soak thesurgical site in a bathtub
* Drive
+ Return to work
+ Return to school
* Remove dressing (keep all surgical dressings and bandages clean, dry and intact)
FOLLOW-UP: Please make an appointment with your surgeon as instructed. You do not need to follow up with your
anesthesiologist unless specifically instructed to doso.
TEMPORARY SYMPTOMS YOU MAY EXPERIENCE AFTER SURGERY:
Decreased appetite, dizziness, drowsiness, slight fever, headache, IV site irritation and tenderness, nausea, procedure site
tenderness, sore throat, difficulty urinating.
Ifyou go home on a narcotic pain medication you may experience constipation. To prevent this increase fluid intake and
take an over the counter stool softener as needed.
SYMPTOMS TO REPORT TO YOUR SURGEON:
* Foul smelling drainage, increased redness, red streaking, swelling, tenderness at or from the incision site
* Continuous nausea and vomiting
* Severe or persistent headache
Fever of 101.0 or greater
Heavy bleeding from the site
Severe pain
Inability to urinate
Changes in extremity sensation, color, or temperature
QUESTIONS?: Please call your surgeon with any questions or concerns. Ifproblems arise outside normal business hours
contact thesurgeon's office and follow prompts to page your physician. In the event of an emergency dial 911.

Important Message from Medicare
Your Rights as a Hospital Inpatient:
You can receive Medicare covered services. This inchdes medically necessary hospital services and
Services you may need after you are discharged, if ordered by your doctor. You have a right to know
about these services, who will pay for them, and where you can getthem
You can be involved in any decisions about your hospital stay.
You can any concems you have about the quality ofcare you receive to your QIO at:KEPRO 1-
[PHONE-546]. The QIO is the independent reviewer authorized byMedicare to review the decision to
discharge you.
You can work with the hospital to prepare for your safe discharge and arrange for services youmay
need affer you leave the hospital When you no longer need inpatient hospital care, your doctor or the
hospital staff will inform you ofyour planned discharge date,
You can speak with your doctor or other hospital staff if you have concerns about being discharged.
Your Right to Appeal Your Hospital Discharge:
You have the right to an immediate, independent medical review (appeal) of the decision to discharge
you from the hospital If you dothis, you will not have to pay for the services you receive during the
appeal (except for charges like copays and deductibles).
Ifyou choose toappeal, the independent reviewer will ask for your opinion, The reviewer also will look
at your medical records and/or other relevant information. You do not have to prepare anything in
writing, but you have the right to do so if you wish.
If you choose to appeal, you and the reviewer will each receive a copy ofa detailed explanation about
why your covered hospital stay should not contime. You will receive this detailed notice only after you
request anappeal
Ifthe QIO finds that you are not ready to be discharged from the hospital, Medicare will contime to
cover your hospital services.
If the QIO agrees services should no longer becovered after the discharge date, neither Medicare nor
your Medicare health plan will pay for your hospital stay after noon of the day after the QIO notifies you
ofitsdecision. If you stop services no later than that time, you will avoid financial liability.
If you do not appeal, you may have to pay for any services you receive affer your discharge date.
See page 2 of this notice for more information.

How toAsk For anAppeal of your Hospital Discharge
e You must make your request to the QIO listed above.
* Your request for an appeal should be made as soon as possible, but no later than your planned discharge
date and before you leave the hospital
e The QIO will notify you ofits decision as soonas possible, generally no later than 1dayafter it receives
all necessary information
¢ Call the QIO listed _on Page 1to appeal, or if you have questions.
IfYou Miss The Deadline toRequest An Appeal, You May HaveOther Appeal Rights:
e Ifyou have Original Medicare: Call the QIO listed on Page 1.
¢ If you belong to a Medicare health plan: Call your plan at @PTINSPAYOR@ @INSCVGPHONE@.

TRANSFUSION OF BLOOD, BLOOD COMPONENTS & RH IMMUNE GLOBULIN
Consent: Your. doctor may determine that a blood transfusion is needed as part of your medical. care. It is important that you or your legal
representative understand the risks and benefits of receiving a blood. component transfusion. Your doctor will explain (or has explained) to
you or your legal representative the risks and benefits of a blood component transfusion and any alternative treatment(s) to a blood
transfusion, The following information on blood and blood components is being given to you or your legal representative to assist you or
your legal representative in making an informed decision about receiving a blood transfusion. Before you or your legal representative sign
the informed consent to receive a blood transfusion, your docter or medical professional will be available to answer any questions you
may have about blood and blood transfusions. Please ask these individuals to explain anything that you did not understand about the
risks and benefits of the blood transfusion you may require.
Benefits: Tests performed during your hospitalization may show that you need blood. Blood is made of many elements. Which elements
you should get and the amount fo be given.are decisions made only by your doctor.
*Red Blood cells - carry oxygen to your tissue and organs
*Granulocytes - white cells needed to.fight infection
*Platelets - help clot your blood
*Cryoprecipitate -Several proteins needed to clot your blood
*Plasma -._liquid portion assists inclotting your blood
Testing the blood you receive: Volunteer donors, that undergo strict screening, donate blood. Many tests (in addition to the blood type)
are performed on this blood and are tested by the blood supplier:
* Syphilis (a sexually transmitted disease)
* Hepatitis B and C (viruses that injure the liver)
* HIV (viruses that cause AIDS)
* HTLV (human T-cell lymphotrophic viruses which may cause blood and nerve
disease)
*WNV West Nile Virus (virus that may cause Encephalitis)
Any blood donation found to be positive forany of these tests is destroyed. Finally, ifyou are going to receive red blood cells, the hospital
blood bank will test a sample of your blood and that ofthe acceptable donated blood unit to make sure that the blood is compatible
(crossmatching your blood).
Risks: Blood should be thought of as a drug having certain risks or side effects. Some patients may have a mild reaction to a blood
transfusion such as fever, chills, hives or body aches. More severe reactions such as breathing difficulties and shock occur very rarely.
Human error, although uncommon, may also occur during preparation of your blood component for transfusion.
The risk ofgetting aninfectious disease such as hepatitis or HIV from.a blood transfusion is very rare.
Infectious Agent Estimated Risk (per unit) As compared with therisks with voluntary or involuntary activities:
e Hepatitis B virus 1:280,000 Activity Risks (death/person/year)
* Hepatitis C virus 1:1,149,000 * Smoking 1:200
. Other (malaria, chagas, babesiosis) 1:1,000,000 * Automobile Driving 1;10,000
* — HIV 1:1,467,000 ¢ Tornado 1:455,000
* HTLV 1:641,000-1,900,000 ° influenza 1:5,000
+ West Nile Virus 1:7,000;000
Alternative T: 2
*Autologous blood transfusion refers to procedures in which you may serve asyour own blood. donor..In.some.-cases, this may bedone
“prior to your hospitalization: Autologous units are reserved foryour use and kept until the expiration date ifnot used by you. They cannot
ever be given to another person if not used by you. If this blood is insufficient for your transfusion needs, then blood components from
other donors will be used,
* In other cases, blood lostduringsurgery can be washed and given back toyou, (called intraoperativecell salvage or'cell saver). Those |
‘with religious objections can optfor a cell saver with a continuous closed circuit.
* In other situations, you may be able to: select the donors of your blood (family or friends) and receive a directed donation. Studies have
shown, however, that thesedonations are nosafer than that from the community blood supply. Directed units that you do not use will be
released on the day of your hospital discharge for use by other patients unless your physician notifies the blood bank to hold those units
foryour use in the future or until the expiration date.
* Bloodless medicine/surgery techniques (called Blood Conservation) may be appropriate for sel d pati in certain circumstances.
